criterion_breakdown.label: Shell-law fidelity across native embodiment
criterion_breakdown.note: It is the only option that cleanly treats native as an embodiment of the governed shell law rather than a browser imitation.

criterion_breakdown.label: Server-authoritative legality
criterion_breakdown.note: The source blueprint explicitly frames this as a server-authoritative projection-and-command client, which the native Xcode approach matches directly.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance for large tables, diffs, and investigations
criterion_breakdown.note: SwiftUI plus targeted AppKit bridges can outperform the browser meaningfully on dense audit, diff, and evidence workflows.

criterion_breakdown.label: Auth/session safety and system-browser handoff quality
criterion_breakdown.note: System-browser auth, Keychain-backed session artifacts, and native return-target control are first-class here.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cache isolation and invalidation integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: A dedicated native architecture can encode FE-25 and FE-75 exactly rather than adapting browser cache assumptions.

criterion_breakdown.label: Scene/window coherence and restoration behavior
criterion_breakdown.note: WindowGroup, scene restoration, and detached support surfaces map directly to the blueprint's scene law.

criterion_breakdown.label: Native ergonomics and OS integration
criterion_breakdown.note: It unlocks true menu commands, Quick Look, native notifications, keyboard flows, and deep multi-window work.

criterion_breakdown.label: Release, signing, notarization, and compatibility operability
criterion_breakdown.note: This is the only option that fully embraces signing, notarization, hardened runtime, and kill-switch posture as native product concerns.

criterion_breakdown.label: Implementation velocity and long-term maintainability
criterion_breakdown.note: The Xcode-native path costs more upfront, but it gives the clearest long-term ownership seams and avoids wrapper debt.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testing strategy fit across native and browser-owned handoffs
criterion_breakdown.note: It supports a clean XCUITest plus Playwright split instead of forcing all behavior through one compromised runtime.

alternatives.label: Signed/notarized Xcode-native macOS client with SwiftUI-first delivery and targeted AppKit acceleration
risks: Requires deliberate Xcode workspace, signing, kill-switch, and bridge-governance discipline rather than one thin wrapper runtime.
strengths: Best fit for system-browser auth, Keychain session posture, multi-window deep work, and high-density operator investigation.
Preserves server-authoritative legality while still giving the desktop product real native advantages over the browser.
alternatives.summary: Ship a first-class internal operator desktop app in Xcode, using SwiftUI for shell composition and AppKit only where density or performance justifies it.

criterion_breakdown.label: Shell-law fidelity across native embodiment
criterion_breakdown.note: A wrapper runtime makes it too easy to re-skin browser metaphors instead of building real native scene law.

criterion_breakdown.label: Server-authoritative legality
criterion_breakdown.note: It can stay server-authoritative, but browser-local habits and wrapper shortcuts make client-local drift more tempting.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance for large tables, diffs, and investigations
criterion_breakdown.note: It helps a little with packaging, but it still inherits many browser rendering limits on the exact workloads native is meant to improve.

criterion_breakdown.label: Auth/session safety and system-browser handoff quality
criterion_breakdown.note: System-browser and embedded-wrapper boundaries become blurrier and harder to keep exact.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cache isolation and invalidation integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Wrapper-local persistence and browser-style caches make FE-25 and FE-75 discipline harder to keep sharp.

criterion_breakdown.label: Scene/window coherence and restoration behavior
criterion_breakdown.note: Detached support windows and restoration are possible, but the model still pulls toward tabbed web thinking.

criterion_breakdown.label: Native ergonomics and OS integration
criterion_breakdown.note: Some desktop APIs are available, but the product remains semantically browser-first rather than native-first.

criterion_breakdown.label: Release, signing, notarization, and compatibility operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Desktop packaging exists, but hardened-runtime and entitlement posture are weaker fits than a true Xcode-native app.

criterion_breakdown.label: Implementation velocity and long-term maintainability
criterion_breakdown.note: Short-term velocity is real, but long-term wrapper debt and performance compromises accumulate quickly.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testing strategy fit across native and browser-owned handoffs
criterion_breakdown.note: The automation split is muddier because the product never cleanly commits to native or browser ownership.

alternatives.label: Electron, Tauri, or browser-wrapper desktop delivery
risks: Encourages a browser-wrapper mindset, weakens true native scene/window design, and makes system-browser/session boundaries easier to blur.
strengths: Faster initial implementation because web surfaces can be reused aggressively.
Can centralize some browser and desktop development around one shared rendering stack.
alternatives.summary: Deliver the operator desktop product primarily as a browser runtime wrapped in a desktop shell with light native integrations.

criterion_breakdown.label: Shell-law fidelity across native embodiment
criterion_breakdown.note: It avoids desktop drift only by refusing to solve native embodiment at all.

criterion_breakdown.label: Server-authoritative legality
criterion_breakdown.note: Browser-only delivery can keep legality centralized, which is its strongest attribute.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance for large tables, diffs, and investigations
criterion_breakdown.note: It leaves the heaviest operator workflows stuck with browser constraints that the native blueprint exists to overcome.

criterion_breakdown.label: Auth/session safety and system-browser handoff quality
criterion_breakdown.note: Browser auth is familiar, but it gives up the safer system-browser-plus-Keychain posture for the operator desktop product.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cache isolation and invalidation integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Browser cache isolation can be good, but it does not solve native restoration or OS-artifact control because there is no native product.

criterion_breakdown.label: Scene/window coherence and restoration behavior
criterion_breakdown.note: There is no true answer for scene restoration, detached support windows, or native multi-window continuity because the product never ships them.

criterion_breakdown.label: Native ergonomics and OS integration
criterion_breakdown.note: It forfeits Quick Look, native menus, rich keyboard flows, and deep multi-window investigation altogether.

criterion_breakdown.label: Release, signing, notarization, and compatibility operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Operationally simpler because there is no native signing or notarization surface to run.

criterion_breakdown.label: Implementation velocity and long-term maintainability
criterion_breakdown.note: It is cheaper upfront, though that savings comes from declining the native product requirement.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testing strategy fit across native and browser-owned handoffs
criterion_breakdown.note: One browser stack is easier to test, but it leaves the native handoff and restoration problem unsolved rather than solved well.

alternatives.label: Browser-only delivery with no first-class native desktop product
risks: Rejects the product requirement for keyboard-first, multi-window, high-density native investigation work and leaves browser limits in place.
strengths: Operationally simpler because no signed native product, update channel, or Xcode workspace is required.
Maintains one rendering stack and one test surface.
alternatives.summary: Keep operators entirely in browser deployables and do not ship a dedicated macOS workspace.

criteria.label: Shell-law fidelity across native embodiment
criteria.rationale: Native delivery must preserve the same object, shell, support-region, and recovery law instead of inventing a fourth desktop-only shell.

criteria.label: Server-authoritative legality
criteria.rationale: The desktop app must remain a projection-and-command client rather than a second compliance engine.

criteria.label: Performance for large tables, diffs, and investigations
criteria.rationale: Native delivery is only worth the cost if it materially outperforms the browser on high-density operator work.

criteria.label: Auth/session safety and system-browser handoff quality
criteria.rationale: Native sign-in and step-up must use system-browser-managed flows with safe return-target continuity and no raw authority credentials on device.

criteria.label: Cache isolation and invalidation integrity
criteria.rationale: Native speed is lawful only when FE-25 and FE-75 boundaries are explicit, exact, and fail closed on drift.

criteria.label: Scene/window coherence and restoration behavior
criteria.rationale: Primary scenes, detached support windows, and restoration rules must stay concrete enough that later native work does not drift.

criteria.label: Native ergonomics and OS integration
criteria.rationale: A macOS product should use menu commands, keyboard-first flows, Quick Look, state restoration, and multi-window deep work instead of mimicking browser habits.

criteria.label: Release, signing, notarization, and compatibility operability
criteria.rationale: Native delivery needs a sane operational model for signing, hardened runtime, kill switches, compatibility windows, and browser fallback.

criteria.label: Implementation velocity and long-term maintainability
criteria.rationale: The chosen strategy must be implementable by a native team without locking the product into a permanently costly abstraction mistake.

criteria.label: Testing strategy fit across native and browser-owned handoffs
criteria.rationale: The architecture must pair XCUITest and native preview coverage with Playwright over browser-owned handoff surfaces.
